observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

   a. Municipal Accountant (i) set-up the receivables for RPT and SET at the
      beginning of the year based on the certified list showing the name  of
      taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for  the  year  from  the
      Municipal Treasurer; (ii) recognize income in the period it was earned
      and  not  when  collected;  (iii)  record  only  the  share   of   the
      Municipality and Barangays from the payment of RPT  and  SET  made  to
      PTO; (iv) reconcile its records with the books of the PGOrM for proper
      recognition of the RPT and SET due from the province; (v) analyze  the
      net effect of the foregoing observations in the  financial  statements
      and draw the necessary adjusting entries in the  General  and  Special
      Education Fund books to reflect the correct balance  of  the  affected
      accounts; and (vi) henceforth, ensure proper drawing of  JEVs  in  the
      recording of income, collection, deposit and transfer of  current  and
      delinquent basic RPT and SET; and

   b. Municipal Treasurer  (i)  furnish  the  Municipal  Accountant  at  the
      beginning of the year a certified list showing the name  of  taxpayers
      and the amount due and collectible for the year for the  establishment
      of Real Property Tax  Receivables/Special  Education  Tax  Receivables
      based on the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card,
      and (ii) ensure the correct reporting of collection  and  distribution
      of RPT collection in Form 60.

We recommended that the:


   a. Municipal Accountant ensure  that  the  procurement  and  issuance  of
      supplies and materials  be  recorded  in  the  appropriate  asset  and
      expense accounts pursuant to the asset method of accounting prescribed
      under Section 114 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I;


   b. Municipal Accountant and GSO implement the perpetual inventory  method
      for accounting  the  supplies  and  inventories,  maintain  subsidiary
      records for inventory in the form of supplies ledger cards  and  stock
      cards, respectively, and ensure  regular  reconciliation  of  the  two
      records in accordance with the same regulation; and


   c. Inventory Committee prepare the RPCI at the end of every semester  and
      submit it to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January  31  of
      each year.

We recommended that the:


   a. General Services Officer  and  the  Inventory  Committee  prepare  and
      submit the RPCPPE and RPCLRN pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual  on
      the NGAS for LGUs, Volume  I  and  COA  Circular  No.  2015-008  dated
      November 23, 2015 and reconcile the  same  with  the  records  of  the
      Municipal Accountant, and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor
      on or before January 31 of each year;


   b. Municipal Accountant analyze the nature of Disaster  Risk  and  Rescue
      Equipment account and prepare necessary adjustments,  and  submit  the
      same to this Office; and


   c. General Services Officer remove the name of the Municipal Mayor in all
      the vehicles of the Municipal Government pursuant to Section 2.2.7  of
      COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30,  2013,  and  apprise  this
      Office of the action taken thereto.

We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant  make  appropriate
   adjustments for the misclassified obligations to officers  and  employees
   of ₱7,107,966.38 to the Due to Officers and Employees  account,  balances
   of fund transfers from various GOCCs  of  ₱701,748.06  to  Due  to  GOCCs
   account, and the excess claim of honorarium amounting  to  ₱47,450.00  to
   Due from Officers and Employees  account  pursuant  to  Annex  B  of  COA
   Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, and submit the copy of  the
   JEV to the Audit Team.

We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant  (i)  analyze  the
   transactions relative to receipt of proceeds, payment to  the  contractor
   of the Phase I project, payment of loan amortization and the  application
   of payments made, and make appropriate adjusting entries  to  record  the
   outstanding balance in the  Loans  Payable  –  Domestic  account  in  the
   General Fund Book; (ii) prepare and maintain Subsidiary  Ledger  (SL)  to
   determine the composition of the Loans Payable account and to  prove  the
   accuracy of its balance as recorded in the General Fund books; and  (iii)
   ensure the  proper  recognition  of  intra-agency  receivables  and  Bank
   Charges accounts in the books of accounts of General Fund books.

We recommended that the:


   a. Municipal  Accountant  and  General  Services  Officer  implement  the
      perpetual inventory method for accounting the various assets held  for
      distribution and maintain subsidiary records for inventory in the form
      of supplies ledger cards and stock  cards,  respectively,  and  ensure
      regular reconciliation of the two records in accordance with the  same
      regulation; and


   b. Municipal Accountant make appropriate  adjustments  in  the  books  of
      accounts to recognize the infrastructure assets and land  procured  by
      the  Municipal   Government,   if   warranted,   and   ensure   proper
      classification of the transaction in accordance with the provision  of
      COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure proper recording
   of transactions related to the expenditures charged against the  Disaster
   Risk Reduction and Management Fund and utilization of fund transfers from
   national government agencies on the recognition of  the  asset,  expense,
   income  and  closing  entries  pursuant  to  the  Fair  Presentation  and
   Compliance with IPSAS under Paragraph 27  of  IPSAS  1,  Presentation  of
   Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant  prepare  the  year-end
   trial balance and financial statements for the 20% Development  Fund  and
   waterworks system, market and slaughterhouse pursuant to Section  110  of
   the Manual.

We recommended that the Municipal  Treasurer  (i)  ensure  that  the
   collections of the collectors are within the maximum cash  accountability
   allowed in  their  fidelity  bond,  otherwise,  increase  the  amount  of
   fidelity bonds to avoid such cases in compliance with  Treasury  Circular
   No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009; (ii)  install  a  more  sound  internal
   control on  the  collection  and  remittance  by  requiring  the  revenue
   collectors to remit their collections daily regardless of the  amount  of
   collections on hand pursuant to COA Circular No.  92-382  dated  July  3,
   1992; and (iii) install proper control over cash tickets of the Municipal
   Government by stamping serial numbers and/or name or logo in each  ticket
   to properly account the issuance and the remittance of the collectors.

We recommended that the:


   a. Municipal Accountant submit the BRS for PHESI DLF and RWMHEEF accounts
      and prepare adjustments to the books of accounts,  if  warranted,  and
      ensure the immediate and timely preparation and submission of BRS  for
      all bank accounts to the Audit Team; and


   b. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer (i) exert diligent effort
      to substantiate the identified reconciling items, and submit the  same
      to this Office, for audit purposes; (ii) reconcile their cash  records
      and settle the disparity in the balances of cash accounts per  GL  and
      cashbooks; and (iii) ensure  that  identified  reconciling  items  are
      immediately analyzed and adjusted in the books of accounts to  present
      fairly the balance of the cash in bank of the Municipal Government.

We recommended that the:


   a. Municipal Accountant analyze and conduct  a  thorough  review  of  the
      Accounts Payable outstanding for more  than  two  years  to  determine
      valid claims and facilitate the reversion of the unsettled obligations
      to the unappropriated surplus as mandated in  Section  98  of  PD  No.
      1445, and  together with the concerned implementing  Offices,  collate
      the documents relative the long outstanding  payables  and  coordinate
      with the source agencies for the proper disposition of the funds;

   b. Municipal Treasurer and other concerned offices  coordinate  with  the
      suppliers and employees to inform  them  of  the  unclaimed  payables,
      otherwise provide certification to  the  Municipal  Accounting  Office
      that there are no valid claimants for the long  outstanding  payables;
      and

   c. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer return the excess  amount
      of the fund received  for  fully  completed  projects  to  the  source
      agencies pursuant to Section 4.9 of  COA  Circular  No.  94-013  dated
      December 13,  1994,  and  provide  appropriate  actions  that  may  be
      undertaken thereon for effective and efficient utilization of the fund
      to ensure that the intended beneficiaries could benefit therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget  Officer  submit  budgetary
   documents authorizing the utilization of the proceeds from the CY 2019 to
   2021 loan pursuant to Section 4(1)  of  PD  No.  1445  as  reiterated  in
   Section 305 (a) of RA No. 7160 and 2016 Budget Operations  Manual  issued
   under Local Budget Circular No. 112 dated June 10, 2016.

We recommended that the:

   a. Municipal Planning and Development  Officer  strictly  adhere  to  the
      manner of presenting the PPAs in the AIP/SIP as prescribed  under  the
      2016 Budget Operations Manual for LGUs and prepare  the  SIP  for  new
      item of expenditures approved by the MDC for adoption by the SB;


   b. Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer submit  the  list  of
      actual recipients of foods packs with the  corresponding  quantity  of
      goods distributed;

   c. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer refrain from payment thru
      reimbursement basis if payments can be made thru check directly to the
      suppliers/creditors and subject to the usual procurement process;

   d. Municipal Treasurer ensure that disbursement vouchers  and  supporting
      documents are stamped “paid” after the payee claimed the check; and

   e. Municipal Planning and Development Officer, Municipal  Accountant  and
      Municipal  Disaster  Risk  Reduction  and  Management  Officer  submit
      satisfactory  justification  on  noted  observation  relative  to  the
      utilization of COVID-19 funds of the Municipality.

We recommended that the:

   a. BAC Chairperson and BAC  Secretariat  provide  justification  for  the
      observations on the procurement of infrastructure projects and  goods.
      Likewise, ensure compliance with the relevant provision  of  the  2016
      Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and  GPPB  issuances  such  as  sending  of
      invitation letters to observers, proper format of  the  omnibus  sworn
      statement and bid security, the timeline in the execution of  contract
      agreement, posting of NOA, contract and NTP in the  PhilGEPS  website,
      LGU website and at any conspicuous place within  LGU  premises  within
      the  prescribed  period,  posting  of  warranty  security,   supported
      variation orders and compliance with GPPB  Resolution  013-2005  dated
      August 5, 2005, and use the 6th  edition  of  the  Philippine  Bidding
      Documents as provided in GPPB Circular No. 04-2020 dated September 16,
      2020 and GPPB Resolution No. 16-2020 dated September  16,  2020  among
      others; and

   b. Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and  BAC  Chairperson  submit
      the (i) justification on the noted observation in the procurement  and
      implementation of the projects, (ii) lacking supporting  documents  in
      compliance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001
      dated June 14, 2012 and pertinent provisions of the Revised IRR of  RA
      No. 9184 and (iii) evidence to substantiate compliance with  Memoranda
      of Agreement for audit purposes.

We recommended that the:

   a. Municipal Budget Officer provide justification on the presentation  of
      the account name, such as medical dental and  laboratory  expense  and
      welfare goods, in the budget for the 20% Development Fund  instead  of
      the proposed PPAs and how they identify the transactions that  can  be
      charged therein; and

   b. Municipal Development Council and other  concerned  officials  refrain
      from appropriating lump-sum appropriations under the  20%  Development
      Funds and be guided by the proposed priority developmental projects as
      provided by DILG-DBM JMC Nos. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 and 2020-
      01 dated March 27, 2020.

We recommended that the:


   a. MDRRM Officer (i) submit the LDRRMFIP in accordance  with  the  format
      provided in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 in  relation  to  the
      provision of Section 4.0 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March
      25,  2013; (ii) together with the Municipal  Planning  and  Development
      Coordinator  and  MDRRM   Council,   provide   specific,   measurable,
      achievable, realistic and time-bound (SMART) performance indicators in
      each PPAs appropriated for MDRRM Fund to facilitate monitoring of  the
      attainment of the planned PPAs; and (iii) monitor and  facilitate  the
      implementation of the PPAs under the MDRRM Fund and  ensure  that  all
      projects appropriated in MDRRM Fund be implemented during the year;
   b. Municipal Budget Officer provide the breakdown of PPAs  charged  under
      the MDRRMF, indicate the appropriate  expected  outputs  and  properly
      classify the nature of expenditure per PPAs  in the LBPF No. 4;


   c. BAC Chairperson and BAC Secretariat provide justification on the noted
      observations on the procurement of infrastructure projects and  goods.
      Likewise, ensure compliance with the relevant provision  of  the  2016
      Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and GPPB resolutions to avoid doubt on  the
      procurement procedure of the Municipality;


   d.  MDRRM  Officer,  Municipal  Planning  and  Development   Coordinator,
      Municipal   Budget   Officer,   and   Municipal   Accountant   provide
      justification on the noted  observations  relative  to  the  planning,
      budgeting and utilization of the 5% LDRRM Fund; and


   e. MDRRM  Officer  and  the  MSWD  Officer  submit  the  details  of  the
      distributed goods totaling ₱2,736,896.50 and  ensure  that  the  goods
      distributed are properly accounted and tallied with the record of  its
      procurement  and  the  date  of  receipt  of  the  beneficiaries   are
      indicated.

We recommended that the:


   a. Human Resource Management Officer  (i)  submit  justification  on  the
      necessity  of  hiring  459  job-order  and  103  contract  of  service
      personnel as of December 31, 2021; and (ii) conduct a  thorough  study
      and evaluation of the Municipality’s needs for additional personnel to
      avoid excessive hiring  of  individuals  under  contract  of  services
      and/or job orders and the duplication of the work being undertaken  by
      regular employees, and provide the Audit Team a copy  of  such  study;
      and

   b. Municipal Accountant ensure that  paid  payrolls  are  supported  with
      complete documentation pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the GAD Focal  Point
   System:


   a. Instruct the MPDC to maintain GAD database with sex-disaggregated data
      and other relevant data that will serve as basis for gender-responsive
      planning, programming and policy formulation, and aid  in  identifying
      gender issues focused amongst clients and organization;


   b. Prioritize the attendance of the GFPS Executive Committee, TWG and MET
      to various GAD-related trainings and seminars for them to serve  as  a
      catalyst in gender mainstreaming  in  the  Municipality  and  identify
      gender issues in their GPB; and


   c. Instruct the GFPS-TWG and/or the Designated GAD Focal Person,  as  the
      case may be, to submit the GAD Agenda and the DILG-received GAD AR and
      its supporting documents within the prescribed  period  to  facilitate
      the evaluation of the success of the GPB.

We recommended that the:

   a. Municipal Accountant (i) analyze the balances of Due to GSIS,  Due  to
      PagIBIG and Due to Philhealth accounts  and  prepare  adjustments,  if
      warranted,  and  coordinate  with  the  concerned  agencies   of   the
      unremitted balances of each account and make the necessary  remittance
      or adjustments, if any; and (ii)  before  deduction  in  the  payroll,
      ensure that all the requirement of the concerned agencies are  met  by
      the employees to avoid accumulation of unremitted contributions as  at
      year-end; and

   b. HRMO-Designate immediately prepare and forward to the AAO the  notices
      of salary adjustments due to  increment  and  promotion  of employees
      before  the  preparation  of  ARA  so  that  any  adjustments  may  be
      incorporated therein, and submit the ARA to the GSIS within the  first
      week of the month so that the billing of the GSIS will be updated.

We recommended that the: 
Municipal Accountant (i) submit the BRS for the 20% Development Fund bank account in the Development Bank of the Philippines, and prepare adjustments to the books of accounts, if warranted; (ii) ensure the immediate and timely preparation and submission of BRS for all bank accounts to the Audit Team; (iii) conduct detailed review and checking of bank records or reports and request for the necessary documents to support the recording of the reconciling items, if any; and (iv) exert diligent effort to substantiate the identified reconciling items and prepare the necessary adjustments; and 
Municipal Treasurer (i) refund the ₱2,200.00 charged against the Trust Fund account for falling below the required ADB; (ii) monitor regularly the bank balances of all the accounts of Municipality if it complies with the requirements of the bank to avoid incurrence of additional bank charges; (iii) coordinate with the source agencies of the funds maintained in the bank accounts tagged as dormant to determine if the Municipality still have outstanding unliquidated balance and make appropriate action thereto; and (iv) ensure that funds for the tax to be remitted from GF-20% DF and TF-EUF be transferred on or before the date of remittance of the GF-Proper to BIR to avoid possible use of cash allocated for other purposes; and 
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer (i) reconcile their cash records and settle the disparity in the balances of cash accounts per GL and cashbooks; and (ii) revert back to the accounts of the Municipality the derecognized Cash-in-Bank amounting to ₱1,437,538.12, and conduct thorough investigation as to the nature of the transaction starting from the year it first appeared in the record of the Municipality and prepare a report copy furnished the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant prepared the bank reconciliati on statements for the months of September to December 2020. Adjustments on interests were properly made and recorded in General Journal as of September 30, 2021. The Municipal Treasurer refunded the amount of ₱2,200 last October 31, 2021. With regard to the dormant account on trust fund with account number 0502-1105-35, the previous OIC-Accountant confirmed that this was previously existing but without purpose and was consequently used for downloaded funds on Supplementary Feeding. As per records, all SFP funds were properly liquidated, hence, a certificatio n from the Department of Social Welfare and Development was obtained. For the dormant account on trust fund with account number 0502-1065-97, the reason for the non-receipt of the benefits was due to non-issuance of official receipts in their previous downloaded funds. Hence, the Municipal Accountant e-mailed the concern and forwarded the official receipts. The cash balance amounting to ₱1,437,538.12 previously derecognized was reverted and recorded to General Journal.
observations.reason: Due to voluminous workload, reconciliati on of the cashbook and accounting records could not be made. Likewise, particulars of the ₱1,437,538.12 cash balance previously derecognized was reverted and recorded to General Journal. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer (i) reconcile their cash records and settle the disparity in the balances of cash accounts per GL and cashbooks; and (ii) revert back to the accounts of the Municipality the derecognized Cash-in-Bank amounting to ₱1,437,538.12, and conduct thorough investigation as to the nature of the transaction starting from the year it first appeared in the record of the Municipality and prepare a report copy furnished the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the: 
Municipal Treasurer (i) identify the payor/s and nature of payment of the dishonored checks, cancel the triplicate copy of the issued accountable forms, void the payment in the index card of the payors and present the same to the Audit Team for monitoring purposes, and (ii) provide the Municipal Accountant the debit memo and the photocopy of the dishonored checks for appropriate adjustment in Accounting records; and 
Municipal Accountant, upon receipt of the documents from the Municipal Treasurer, make appropriate adjustment in the Accounting records and provide the Audit Team with a copy of the adjustment made.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer was able to locate two of the dishonored checks particularly the PNB Check deposited last January 22, 2019 and DBP Check deposited last July 3, 2020, amounting to P10,734.00 and P717.00, respectively. These were immediately collected and deposited and adjusted in the books.
observations.reason: Due to voluminous workload and lack of details and response from the depository bank, appropriate adjustments to the five checks were not made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the procurement and issuance of supplies and materials be recorded in the appropriate asset and expense accounts pursuant to the asset method of accounting prescribed under Section 114 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and the Municipal Accountant and OIC-GSO (i) implement the perpetual inventory method for accounting the supplies and inventories; (ii) maintain subsidiary records for inventory in the form of supplies ledger cards and stock cards, respectively; and (iii) ensure regular reconciliation of the two records in accordance with the same regulation.
observations.managementAction: Municipal Accountant was able to record the inventory in the purchases of supplies to be stocked.
observations.reason: The GSO is not yet a created department, hence, there were no permanent positions with qualifications placed in the said office. Due to bulk load given to the six staff of GSO, they were not able to accomplish the supplies ledger cards and stock cards.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the: 
Municipal Accountant and OIC- General Service Officer (i) coordinate with each other and prepare supporting schedules to facilitate the reconciliation of the property accounts and establishment of actual existence, ownership, and condition of the properties, and (ii) prepare and maintain appropriate property records and reports for PPE account; and 
Municipal Engineer, Accountant and OIC-GSO exert more effort in locating the documents that would show the complete description and cost segregation of the constructed local road networks during CY 2016 and below.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-General Service Officer was able to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant when it comes to the preparation of PPE Reports. The OIC-GSO accomplished the RPCI and RPCPPE.
observations.reason: Due to lack of supporting documents and previous records before 2016, the report was not completed. Due to workloads of the Municipal Accountant and OIC-GSO, the details to be encoded in the PPE reports, as well as the updating of property tags, were not yet finalized. Since the Municipality is currently implementing projects from the DBP loan, the Municipal Engineer was not able to locate the documents that would show the complete description and cost segregation of the constructed local road networks during CY 2016 and below. Proper classification of Seaport System remained unacted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the: 
Municipal Mayor instruct the Coastal Resource Management Board to provide acceptable justification on the insufficiency and/or lack of appropriation for the repayment of the loan in the Environmental User’s Fee Fund for CYs 2013 to 2020; and the Municipal Accountant collate all the documents relative the loan with the Municipal Development Fund Office, analyze the transactions relative receipt of proceeds, payment to the contractor of the Phase I project, payment of loan amortization and the application of payments made, and make appropriate adjusting entries in the General and Trust Fund books, if warranted, and provide the Audit Team copy of the documents and Journal Entry Vouchers, if any; and 
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer identify if payments made by the Municipality for CYs 2013 to 2020 were already applied to the penalties incurred totaling ₱1,647,225.99 for late payment, if so, identify the persons/s responsible for the incurrence of such charges and cause the refund of said amount, and observe the provision of Section 103 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant made constant follow up on the schedule of payment to be applied in principal and interests from MDFO, and the request of the LGU for restructuring, reduction of interest rates and waiving of penalties had been previously sent to MDFO.
observations.reason: The loan from MDFO was already handled by the Landbank of the Philippines, Calapan Branch. It was disclosed that the approval of the Board was not yet done. They have provided the proposed amortization for restructuring and requested additional details for presentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Collections of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office be coordinated and the Municipal Accountant (i) recognize the discounts for prompt and advance payments of RPT and SET, if any, in the books of accounts of the Municipality in the period to which they accrue; and (ii) reconcile the RPT and SET Receivables with the record of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office and make appropriate adjustments, if warranted, and (iii) ensure that collection of SET be accrued to SEF books on the period they were earned instead of recording in the period the cash was actually transferred.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to (a) recognize the discounts for prompt and advance payments of RPT and SET and penalties, if any, in the books of accounts of the Municipality in the period to which they accrue and (b) reconcile the RPT and SET Receivables with the record of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office. The Municipal Accountant was able to gather all Forms 60 starting the year 2009 and list down the amount of discounts and penalties since then. After analysis, appropriate adjustment was made through the general journal, for the General Fund and SEF.
observations.reason: Due to voluminous taxpayers and the limited number of treasury personnel, the collection of the collectors exceeds their maximum cash accountability as stated in their bond. Likewise, due to the distance of the field collectors, regular remittance of the collections of the collectors could not be made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (i) ensure that all collections be deposited intact daily with authorized depositary bank or not later than the next banking day pursuant to Section 69 (1) of PD No. 1445 and Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992 to avoid accumulation of cash in his possession; and (ii) coordinate with the GSO or the Office of the Mayor, as case maybe, ahead of time to accommodate his request for service vehicle, and (iii) ensure that all collections be deposited intact with authorized depositary bank daily or not later than the next banking day pursuant to Section 69 (1) of PD No. 1445 and Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992 to avoid accumulation of cash in his possession.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer agreed to comply with the recommendation.
observations.reason: Due to voluminous taxpayers and the limited number of treasury personnel, the collection of the collectors exceeds their maximum cash accountability stated in their bond. Likewise, due to the distance of the field collectors, regular remittance of the collections of the collectors could not be made.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the: 
Municipal Accountant (i) reconcile with the records of the Audit Team and cause the refund of the excessive payment of travelling expenses; (ii) review and examine thoroughly the claims of Municipal officials and employees so that the excessive claims will be refund; and (iii) ensure compliance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 pertaining to complete documentation for claims.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant informed the employees and officials with excessive claims of travel expenses to refund. The Disbursing Officer certified that the employees

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Treasurer (i) make representation with the LBP to request for the
bank statements in case of delay of its receipt; (ii) be cautious in the collection of
payment through checks, and maintain strict monitoring in tracing of payors of
dishonored checks; and (iii) provide details for the adjustments taken in the
cashbooks;
b. Municipal Accountant (a) exert more efforts to prepare and submit the bank
reconciliation statements, and (b) draw JEV to record all valid and supported
reconciling items and adjustments that require correction in the books; and
c. Municipal Accountant and Treasurer seek assistance from the Executive and
Legislative Departments for the undetermined/unexplained amount of
₱1,437,538.12 for appropriate action.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer cause the
refund of the excess claim of DTE totaling ₱8,050.00. In case of non-compliance
by the listed Accountable Officers (AOs), cause the withholding of their salary
until full settlement of their accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal officials and employees with cash
advances liquidate their CAs within the prescribed period pursuant to Section
5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that the PDO II refund the claimed inland transportation
amounting to ₱979.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require all concerned
Municipal officials and employees to submit the lacking documents and ensure
that all liquidation reports are submitted and duly supported with proper
documentations to establish their legality, validity and propriety.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Mayor review the Municipality’s staffing pattern and the employees’
duties and responsibilities, and make appropriate measures to address the lack
of manpower and voluminous work loads of employee/s who are assigned in the
property management;
b. Inventory, Appraisal and Disposal Committee (i) accomplish the RPCPPE
pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
(ii) conduct physical inventory and valuation of all known Road Network
System within the Municipality and thereafter prepare the RPCLRN for CYs
2016 and below in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated
November 23, 2015;
c. GSO Designate prepare Property Card for all PPE accounts;
d. Inventory, Appraisal and Disposal Committee and the Municipal Accountant
reconcile the result of the physical count with their respective records and
immediately verify and adjust any discrepancies noted; and
e. Municipal Engineer exert more effort in locating the documents that would
show the complete description and cost segregation of the constructed local road
networks during CY 2016 and below.

We recommended that the:
a. BAC strictly adhere to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184
for the procurement of infrastructure projects specifically on the (i) approval of
the HOPE on the BAC recommendation within a period not exceeding 15
calendar days from the determination by the BAC of the bidder with the LCRB,

contractor/supplier pursuant to Section 62 of the law; and (iii) prescribed
format of the BSD and OSS under GPPB Resolution No. 15-2014 and Section
25.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;
b. BAC and Municipal Engineer submit satisfactory explanation on the
inconsistencies noted; and
c. Municipal Accountant, BAC Chairman and Municipal Engineer submit the
lacking necessary supporting documents required under Item 9.0 of COA
Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to support the propriety and validity
of disbursements made.

and strictly comply with (i) GPPB Resolution No. 23-2017 dated May 30, 2017
on the use of the 5th Edition of the Philippine Bidding Documents for the
procurement of infrastructure projects, goods and consulting service; (ii) DO
No. 13, series of 1998, and DOLE AO No. 152, series of 2011 regarding the
requirement of CHSP duly approved by DOLE; and (iii) Sections 25.2 and
37.1.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 on the submission of proof of
ownership of equipment pledged to the contract and approval of the HOPE on
the BAC recommendation;
b. Municipal Accountant comply with Section 1 of LOI 968 dated December 17,
1979;
c. Municipal Mayor and Municipal Engineer adhere to the prescribed period of
issuance of the Certificate of Final Acceptance provided under RA No. 9184; and
d. BAC Chairman, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant submit the
lacking necessary supporting documents required under Item 9.0 of COA
Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to support the propriety and validity
of disbursements made.

We recommended that the:
a. GSO-Designate comply with the provisions under COA Circular No. 2014-002
in the (i) preparation and maintenance of inventory list of procured items based
on the updated SCs and PCs; and (ii) issuance of relief goods to be supported
with the RIS;
b. Municipal Accountant (i) record the purchases and issuances of relief goods
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2014-002; and (ii) include the required
disclosures in the Notes to FS of information pertaining to DRRM; and
c. Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and other Municipal
Officials/employees concerned submit the official receipt and duly-signed IAR
as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to support
the propriety and validity of disbursement made.

ensuring its compliance with Sections 100, 101 and 272 of RA No. 7160 and DepEd-
DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017 on the utilization of SEF to priority items in order to
maximize the benefits that the public school students could derived therefrom.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Mayor and Sangguniang Bayan of the Municipality provide
explanation to justify that the scholarship grant is compliant with the public
purpose requirement under Section 4(2) of PD No. 1445 and the limitations set
forth in Section 447 (a) (5) (xi) of RA No. 7160;
b. Municipal Mayor formulate clear guidelines as to how its constituents may avail
of the scholarship grant, the selection process and requirements thereof subject
to the aforementioned public purpose requirement and limitations; and
c. Sangguniang Bayan observe strict compliance with Section 447 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create a Committee specifically
tasked to formulate a well-defined written policy and guidelines on the hiring of JO
and COS personnel.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the GAD Focal
Person:
a. Prioritize the attendance of the GFPS Executive Committee, TWG,
Secretariat and MET to various GAD-related trainings and seminars in
order for them to help accelerate the gender mainstreaming in the
Municipality;
b. Comply with the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-
01 dated July 18, 2013, as amended by JMC No. 2016-01 dated January
12, 2016 and COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014 on the
timelines in the preparation and submission of GPB and GAR; and
c. Require the GAD-IAC to prepare the IRR of the CY 2015 GAD Code of
the Municipality to effectively implement the provisions contained
therein.

We commended the Municipal Accounting and Municipal Treasury Offices
for their continuous adherence to the pertinent provisions of pertinent laws and the
prompt deduction of GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth contributions and other
amount due to these agencies and the remittance thereof within the prescribed
period. We however, recommended that the Municipal Accountant analyze the Due
to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and Philhealth accounts for final disposition of accounts due to
these agencies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Finance Committee (LFC) observe strict compliance with the PS limitation as mandated by Section 325(a) of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160 in the preparation of annual/ supplemental budgets. The Local Chief Executive should always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements are complied with. He should have the political will to allow only the payment of personal services costs within PS limitation. 

Require the refund of the step increments and Performance Incentive Bonus (PIB) amounting to ₱271,000.00 granted to municipal officials and employees in excess of PS limitation and in contrary to Joint Civil Service Commission (CSC) – Department of Budget and Management (DBM) Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2012.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer refrain from disbursing funds from any of the items of appropriation declared inoperative, disallowed or reduced by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan.

Likewise, the Municipal Budget Officer adopt measures to properly monitor the balance of appropriations for this will serve as basis in certifying as to the availability of appropriations and allotments against which expenditures and obligations may be properly charged.
To increase allowed PS level in the ensuing years and avoid the excess in PS Limitation, the Municipal Mayor and the LFC should devise plan/programs to (a) increase its income such as intensifying tax collection efficiency and investing in revenue-generating projects or activities and            (b) reduce PS expenditures by minimizing the hiring of additional personnel especially if their services are not crucial to the agency or their functions are just duplication of functions of existing personnel. 

Lastly, strong support of the Municipal Officials concerned for a continuing tight control of all expenditures specifically to personal services is enjoined.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Finance Committee (LFC) observe strict compliance with the PS limitation as mandated by Section 325(a) of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160 in the preparation of annual/ supplemental budgets. The Local Chief Executive should always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements are complied with. He should have the political will to allow only the payment of personal services costs within PS limitation. 

Require the refund of the step increments and Performance Incentive Bonus (PIB) amounting to ₱271,000.00 granted to municipal officials and employees in excess of PS limitation and in contrary to Joint Civil Service Commission (CSC) – Department of Budget and Management (DBM) Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2012.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer refrain from disbursing funds from any of the items of appropriation declared inoperative, disallowed or reduced by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan.

Likewise, the Municipal Budget Officer adopt measures to properly monitor the balance of appropriations for this will serve as basis in certifying as to the availability of appropriations and allotments against which expenditures and obligations may be properly charged.

To increase allowed PS level in the ensuing years and avoid the excess in PS Limitation, the Municipal Mayor and the LFC should devise plan/programs to (a) increase its income such as intensifying tax collection efficiency and investing in revenue-generating projects or activities and            (b) reduce PS expenditures by minimizing the hiring of additional personnel especially if their services are not crucial to the agency or their functions are just duplication of functions of existing personnel. 

Lastly, strong support of the Municipal Officials concerned for a continuing tight control of all expenditures specifically to personal services is enjoined.
